title: Should libraries lend out tools, seeds, and instruments — not just books?
summary: Argue for or against expanding what public libraries lend beyond books.
topic: Whether public libraries should lend more than books
prompt: Write a short argumentative essay (200–280 words) on whether public libraries should lend tools, seeds, and musical instruments alongside books. Take a clear side. Use at least one concession ('There are, of course, objections…') and one cleft or 'what' construction. Avoid empty fillers like 'in conclusion'.
hintStarters: The image of a library as a quiet room…
What I find compelling about the model is…
There are, of course, objections worth taking seriously.
If a city can lend you…
sampleText:
The image of a library as a quiet room full of books is, in some cities, already out of date. Across the Netherlands, Argentina, and parts of the American Midwest, public libraries have begun lending out drills, sewing machines, garden seeds, and even violins. The idea sounds modest, but it deserves to be taken seriously, because what is really being shared is not the object itself but the right to try something new without paying for the privilege of failing.

What I find compelling about the model is its quiet democracy. A young person who wants to build a bookshelf no longer has to choose between giving up the project or buying a drill they will use twice. A grandmother who suspects her grandchild might love the cello does not need to commit to a six-hundred-euro instrument before that suspicion has been tested. The library, by absorbing the cost of the first attempt, turns curiosity into something affordable.

There are, of course, objections worth taking seriously. Tools wear out faster than books; seeds expire; instruments need tuning. Critics argue that public budgets should not subsidise hobbies. That argument is not unreasonable, but it underestimates what libraries already do. They have never been merely about books — they have been about access to the means of becoming someone slightly different from who you were yesterday.

If a city can lend you the thirty-third translation of a novel you might not finish, it can lend you a hammer.

sampleAnnotations.meaningVi: tiếp cận đến các phương tiện

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft for emphasis
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'What is really being shared is not the object itself but the right to try…' đặt phần quan trọng nhất vào cuối câu để nhấn mạnh.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'What is really being shared is not the object itself but the right to try…' puts the most important idea at the end of the sentence for emphasis.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Concession before counter-argument
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'There are, of course, objections worth taking seriously' thừa nhận phía bên kia trước khi bác bỏ — kỹ thuật chuẩn của bài luận quan điểm.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'There are, of course, objections worth taking seriously' admits the other side before refuting it — a standard opinion-essay technique.

sampleTranslationVi:
Hình ảnh một thư viện như một căn phòng yên tĩnh đầy sách, ở một số thành phố, đã lỗi thời. Tại Hà Lan, Argentina, và một vài vùng ở trung tây Hoa Kỳ, các thư viện công cộng đã bắt đầu cho mượn máy khoan, máy may, hạt giống vườn, thậm chí cả đàn violin. Ý tưởng nghe có vẻ khiêm tốn, nhưng nó đáng được xem trọng, bởi điều thật sự được chia sẻ không phải là vật, mà là quyền được thử một điều mới mà không phải trả tiền cho cái đặc quyền được thất bại.

Điều tôi thấy thuyết phục ở mô hình này là tính dân chủ lặng lẽ của nó. Một bạn trẻ muốn đóng một chiếc kệ sách không còn phải chọn giữa việc bỏ cuộc và việc mua một chiếc khoan mà mình chỉ dùng hai lần. Một người bà nghi ngờ rằng cháu mình có thể yêu cây cello không cần phải cam kết mua một cây đàn sáu trăm euro trước khi điều đó được kiểm chứng. Thư viện, bằng cách gánh chi phí cho lần thử đầu tiên, biến sự tò mò thành điều ai cũng kham được.

Dĩ nhiên, có những phản biện đáng được xem trọng. Công cụ mòn nhanh hơn sách; hạt giống hết hạn; nhạc cụ cần lên dây. Người phản đối nói rằng ngân sách công không nên trợ cấp cho sở thích. Lập luận ấy không phải vô lý, nhưng nó đánh giá thấp những gì thư viện vẫn luôn làm. Thư viện chưa bao giờ chỉ là về sách — nó luôn là về việc tiếp cận các phương tiện để trở thành ai đó hơi khác với chính mình ngày hôm qua.

Nếu một thành phố có thể cho bạn mượn bản dịch thứ ba mươi ba của một cuốn tiểu thuyết mà bạn có thể không đọc hết, nó cũng có thể cho bạn mượn một chiếc búa.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a 'what' cleft construction correctly to add emphasis?
options: What is really being shared is not the object itself but the right to try something new.
What that is really shared, is not the object but the right.
It is what really shared the object and not the right.
Sharing really is not the object what but the right.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What [clause] is [emphasised noun]' is the standard cleft pattern for highlighting the key idea.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the pattern 'What … is …' that delays the key noun for emphasis.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option introduces a concession most naturally before a counter-argument?
options: Of course there is no objection anywhere ever in the world.
There are, of course, objections worth taking seriously.
Objections, of course there is, taking serious.
No critics has ever objected to this idea, of course.
mcQuestions.explanation: Soft acknowledgement of the other side ('of course') signals you've thought about objections before answering them.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option admits the other side exists, before pushing back?

mcQuestions.prompt: Choose the sentence that uses a passive construction most naturally at B2 level.
options: Tools are worn out by faster than books.
Tools wear out faster than books.
Tools have been wearing out by readers faster than books.
Tools are being worn out by faster than books are.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Wear out' here is an intransitive phrasal verb — not passive. The passive constructions in the other options are wrong or redundant.
mcQuestions.hint: Sometimes the active form is more natural than the passive — pick the cleanest one.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option avoids AI-style filler and keeps the ending sharp?
options: In conclusion, libraries are very important institutions in modern society.
It is important to note that public libraries play a key role today.
If a city can lend you the thirty-third translation of a novel you might not finish, it can lend you a hammer.
Throughout history, humans have always relied on shared resources.
mcQuestions.explanation: C is concrete, specific, and tied to the actual argument. The others are generic openings from the 'banned' list.
mcQuestions.hint: Which ending uses a concrete image instead of an abstract platitude?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a relative clause with 'who' or 'that' most cleanly?
options: A grandmother who suspects her grandchild might love the cello does not need to commit to a six-hundred-euro instrument.
A grandmother that her grandchild might love the cello, doesn't need to buy a cello.
A grandmother she suspects her grandchild loves cello, no need a cello to buy.
Grandmother suspecting cello love grandchild commit instrument no.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'A grandmother who suspects…' uses 'who' to attach a clean defining relative clause to a person.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the correct relative pronoun and clause structure for a person.

criticalThinkingQuestion: If your city's library could only add ONE new lendable category next year, what would you choose, and what would the strongest objection be?
